comments.text: I have proven the quartic case of this problem: if $f$ is monic of degree four and all zeros of $f$, counted with multiplicity, lie in the open unit disk, then two of these zeros can be joined inside $$ {z : |f(z)| < 1} $$ by a possibly degenerate polygonal path of length less than $2$. The preprint is available at https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.24875. In addition, using the Aristotle AI based API, the result has been formalized and verified in Lean. I'm currently unsure of how exactly to share the Lean files for reproducibility and scrutiny, but if anyone is interested/or knows how to share them, I'm more than willing to post those.
GPT-5.4 Thinking announces the solution: https://www.overleaf.com/read/bsngbyycrrbd#10ebf2

I'm explicitly not standard checking this one because this problem has a notorious history of being difficult to reason about for LLMs. Nevertheless, preliminary discussion reveals that this claimed solution does not contain the exact previous gap and that it at least has a chance of dealing with Mac Lane labyrinths.

With the new GPT-5.5 release, I've run standard check which decisively found a serious gap. In proof of Lemma 6, the subharmonicity is oddly applied to conclude $$ |\phi'(r)| \le \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} |\phi'(r e^{i \theta})| \,d\theta. $$ (LHS should be at the circle center i.e. $|\phi'(0)|$.) GPT's opinion is that this is not easily repairable. Also, Ho Boon Suan has earlier messaged me with his own check which pointed out this same exact gap.

In [1215], it was found by Mac Lane that the lemniscate $\{z : |f(z)| < 1\}$ can be extremely "winding"; see some examples there. This may challenge potential approaches to positively resolving this problem. On the other hand, one may also try to construct a counterexample, but this does not follow easily either because Mac Lane's argument controls paths but does not control roots. Credit to GPT-5.4 Thinking for discussion.

Although in [1215] the roots all have modulus $1$, rather than all $<1$. I'm not sure how much this changes the problem. EDIT: ah, I guess one can just replace $f(z)$ by $f(2z)$, say, so Mac Lane's constructions are also relevant here.

Thanks for all the comments on the previous draft. Here is an updated document in which I rewrite some of the arguments, as well as expand on some areas in which GPT raised concerns. There are still some areas that could use a bit more exposition and I am still working on this, but this draft looks to me substantially better than the previous one I had posted.
Thanks! I apply standard check again which notes 2 minor issues. I also add an experimental step where I ask GPT for interesting comments on the paper after the check. No idea if that's useful.
Thanks! Here is an update which I think fixes the issues mentioned by GPT. The last section is also quite useful.

Thanks. Since those are minor issues I think we don’t need to re-check. For the wiki, it’ll stay in the pending section for a while to wait for if others have issues or point out related literature. I also did literature search a little which did not find previous solutions.

I'm willing to rate the argument as being plausibly correct. With regards to having publication-level quality of exposition and literature review, the manuscript still needs a lot of work however, on a number of fronts. Much of this is tied to the tendency of current-level AI writing to dwell far too much on routine results (often without citing relevant literature), or technical details, and not enough time emphasizing the high level structure. In this case, the high-level structure is that there are four key results that resolve the Erdos problem, each of which is independently interesting: 1. The EHP component lemma (at least one component $U$ with two or more zeroes). 2. An integral bound $\int_U |f'(z)/f(z)|\ dA \leq 2\pi m$, where $m$ is the number of zeroes (Proposition 4). 3. A lemma asserting that on any given region $U$, there is a tree connecting the $m$ zeroes of $U$ (formed by following the Morse gradient flow on $\log |f|$, basically) of total length at most $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_U |f'(z)/f(z)|\ dA$, at least assuming some technical non-degeneracy hypotheses on $f$. (Proposition 12) 4. A simple combinatorial lemma that asserts that any tree contains a path of length at most twice the total length of the tree, divided by the number of vertices of the tree. (Lemma 17) There is also a technical 0. One can perturb $f$ to obey the non-degeneracy conditions required for the third ingredient to work. (Lemma 14) Combining all these ingredients, the Erdos problem follows. But the AI-generated exposition spends a lot of time on the least interesting parts of this process (e.g., Step 0), and many steps could be proven much more quickly. For instance Lemma 3 is obvious because the level sets of $p$ are algebraic sets of dimension less than 2, and thus immediately of measure zero (e.g., by the Crofton formula). Several pages of argument are used to establish the formula $\int_{\varepsilon < u < t} |\nabla u|^2\ dA = 2\pi m(t-\varepsilon)$ on page 5, but this is just the area formula noting that $f$ is $m$-to-one on $U$, so (a suitable branch of) $\log f$ is also $m$-to-one mapping to a fundamental domain of $\{ \varepsilon < \mathrm{Re} z < t \}$. I also suspect that there is a less convoluted way to arrive at Proposition 4 from this area formula and the Polya inequality than the one provided in the AI-generated text, although I have not tried too hard to locate such a simplified argument. Similarly, the perturbative component of the argument (Step 0) seems to occupy far more space in the AI-generated document than it needs to: I think the cleanest thing to do is first resolve the Erdos problem for "generic" polynomials f (perhaps with some sort of "padding") and use some sort of Rouche theorem argument to deduce the general case from this, instead of spend so much time constructing explicit perturbations of polynomials. Finally, it's a bit concerning that the AI-generated article cites almost none of the extensive literature on lemniscates, and I suspect that many of the arguments here have precursors or other links to the literature that need to be explored; it may be that the AI tool implicitly relied upon such literature arguments without explicitly disclosing the connection. All of this would have to be resolved (preferably by a primarily human-generated document) before this result would be suitable for submission to a peer-reviewed journal.

Thanks to Tao for such detailed review! I agree in general. Though I would caution against concluding that current AI has such-and-such tendency per se, as we have seen that results differ a lot depending on how you work with it. For this one, interestingly the original 13 pages draft (that has more flaws) seems to have more coherent narrative, which I think is the missing quality here. Perhaps the process of trying to correct for mistakes has unintentionally obscured the original narrative? If so, there’s also the option of *starting* from that original draft, using the current longer draft as reference only, and using Tao’s comments as a high-level guide. This may be able to construct a correct and more coherent manuscript. If you do that, I’ll be willing to check it again!

Thanks for the comments! This all looks valid to me, and I'd be happy to construct a more coherent and correct manuscript following these comments. Regarding Tao's last concern about the AI not citing existing research: this is probably due my telling GPT not to search the internet when I gave it this problem - I have found that without this specific instruction, LLMs and specifically GPT have a tendency to see that such a problem is considered open and "give up", rather than make a meaningful effort to solve the problem. I'll look at existing literature on lemniscates and see if there are some existing results that help here or need to be cited.

Yes, some people do use that strategy of not browsing to frame the problem as if it has a known solution or should be easily doable with existing techniques. There are also other ways to coax it, see e.g. qrdl's comment in the AI thread. My way of doing something like this is to present it with a promising approach - so seed it with something first. This gets it started immediately without worrying about the open status. (If you don't have a promising approach you can probably ask it to generate approaches, and then follow through in the same way!) But, from the current situation, I now see that the "not browsing" approach can also have this weakness. In framing the problem as "potentially not open", any *real* difficulty or issue it encounters is likely to be viewed as easily surmountable (since the problem IS not open). When in fact - this may be precisely where the gaps lie!

Presumably this weakness can be mitigated by passing the output to another independent AI instance with the instruction "this claims to be a proof of a well-known open problem; view this claim with scepticism and attempt to find the flaw in this argument, and also find where it should have cited earlier work". I believe this treatment has been applied to other instances of AI output; I'm not sure how much scepticism you have instructed the AI to have in your 'standard check'. Regardless, I'm sure it's still possible for subtle errors to get through any number of AI checks, as seemed to happen in this case.

The standard check is not meant to be comprehensive as that will take a very long time to complete and be impractical. This means it does not represent a confirmation stamp from me; I just hope it's useful for the author! In this case I also consider that the author is also a mathematician (in their bio) and has done some sanity check themselves, as well as inviting others to assess the claim. Regarding this one, it appears that this particular gap is tricky for GPT-5.4 Thinking. I was just uploading the paper and saying that there should be a gap in Prop 12 regarding the use of Lemma 8 - and it did not find it at first even with this information! So in this case the *content* of the gap appears difficult for GPT itself.

The full transcripts of standard checks are always linked. I do not use "scepticism" instruction explicitly, but the first two steps play analogous roles. The first step is to understand the overview of the work. The second step is to come up with places most worth scrutinizing. The third step is to go through them one by one in the usual cases. The final step is to now go through the whole work from start to end. (This is clearly not meant to be comprehensive.) For this particular one Prop 12 was surfaced as spot #1 to check. However, the actual check did not find this particular gap. (From other evidence it now seems that it's difficult for GPT to spot this one irrespective of the surrounding work process!)

The weakness claimed here is about the resulting output, which may hide the mistake (perhaps unintentionally). Since any checking process is fallible, a hidden mistake has more chance of escaping it. Therefore the checking process is not a mitigation by itself.

Regarding the use of "scepticism" framing itself. While it may seem like a good idea, I believe a neutral framing is better. The reason is that a good check also addresses high-level issues, which is quite hard to see if you zoom in locally. In the past, I've had problems where *every* local part is checked - but there's some global or high-level strategy that doesn't quite work out; these require understanding beyond simple local checks. So I'm now prioritizing big pictures somewhat, and it seems that a scepticism framing may encourage it to be more local. So while it may catch more local issues, it could miss the more global major gaps. At least that's what I think.

After working with Claude and doing some literature search, I have a shorter proof of Proposition 4 (Step 2). From the area formula (noting that $f$ is an $m$-fold map from $U$ to the disk) one has $$ \int_U |f'(z)|^2 / |f(z)|^{2-1/m}\ dA(z) = m \int_{D(0,1)} 1/|w|^{2-1/m}\ dA(w) = 2 \pi m^2$$ so by Cauchy--Schwarz it suffices to show that $$ \int_U |f(z)|^{-1/m}\ dA(z) \leq 2\pi.$$ By the layer-cake decomposition it suffices to show that the level sets $\{ z \in U: |f(z)| \leq s \}$ have area at most $\pi s^{2/m}$ for all $0 < s \leq 1$. By Theorem 1 of this paper of Dubinin (a Polya type inequality), this area is at most $s^{2/m}$ times the area of $U$; and by the classical Polya inequality, $U$ has area at most $\pi$. The claim follows. The arguments used here and in the AI-generated article are also rather similar to those in this paper of Crane. I'm going to look at Proposition 12 (Step 3) next. I have a suspicion that one does not need quite so much working out of the topology of the situation, and should be able to proceed by more purely analytic arguments; but I have not digested it yet.

I now believe that there is a serious issue with Proposition 12, in that the topological claims made by the AI are not actually correct. In particular, the invocation of Lemma 8 on page 21 is unjustified, and likely unjustifiable, because the boundary of the sets $S$ outside of flow-line sides will typically be contained in more than two level sets. Here is a ChatGPT Pro explanation of this. (ChatGPT Pro does propose a fix, but I believe it does not work: by removing much longer regions from $\Omega$, one can no longer afford to glue together vertices on the boundary of the regions removed without incurring a much larger length penalty.) I found this by trying to draw a picture of the situation and finding it did not agree with the topological claims; removing small balls around each zero and critical point does not split the domain into strips with the structure of Lemma 8, and more broadly the flow lines do not seem to organize into connected trees which is essential for the entire strategy to work. Any new version of the paper should, at minimum, have some actual pictures (ideally plotted numerically from actual polynomials) to double-check the claims of the AI. (I had initially been rather impressed by the AI's topological arguments, which were reaching all sorts of conclusions that I did not think were possible... but now I think it is because the AI was simply wrong. It appears to me that avoiding errors in topological arguments are a weakness for large language models, who do not have a direct capability for visual intuition.)

Thanks for all this extra work! Uh-oh. Does this undermine the whole argument? ( Comments for myself: The earlier standard check did flag Proposition 12 as #1 risk. "This is the most intricate part of the paper...A small topological mistake here would likely break the proof outright." The check for #1 itself did find one minor issue, but did not catch the strip issue here: "The strip setup itself seems coherent...the proof uses Lemma 8 to get a genuine strip structure." )

I can elaborate a bit further on why I am now pessimistic on the entire approach. The strategy is to try to connect zeroes of $f$ together by performing gradient flow on $u = \log \frac{1}{|f|}$, which is (locally at least) the same thing as working on level curves of the harmonic conjugate $\mathrm{arg} f$, and hoping that this creates a tree of short total length that one can then cut up (along an Eulerian path) to find a path connecting two zeroes together. The problem is that this flow simply does not create trees, which becomes obvious once one generates a visual plot. Here is a plot of what the gradient flow lines look like for $f(z) = z^2-1$; the construction given is to basically take two arcs of the same color, which connect the two zeroes $\pm 1$ to the boundary of the region $U$ under consideration, but do not connect to each other. There is a critical point at $0$ that can connect the zeroes together, but only two flow lines go into that point and two flow lines go out, and a random flow line construction will never find this. (The integral $\int_U |f'/f|\ dA$, by the way, can be viewed as the average value of the total flow line length for a randomly chosen color.)

Many thanks!! Interestingly, GPT says that the strategy should still go through for $f(z) = z^2 - r^2$. It says that plot's interpretation is accurate, but that the actual strategy is not "random" in that way. I'm now not sure whether it's right or wrong. You may want to see what it says for yourself ...
I would challenge any AI tasked with making this strategy work to actually generate a picture of how this tree is plausibly going to look like.
GPT thinks the tree looks like this.

OK, that was a better attempt than I expected, but it does show that the only flow lines coming from the zeroes that are of plausible use are the ones pointing towards the critical point; the majority of flow lines emenating away from the zeroes will not be useful for this purpose. On the other hand, the integral expression $\int_U |f'/f|\ dA$ is almost entirely dominated by contributions from these "useless" flow lines, particularly if one shrinks the disk around the critical point down from the exaggerated size shown in the AI plot in order to reduce the excess length coming from the degree three vertex of the tree inside that disk. So if this strateegy is to work, one needs to obtain control on something other than these sorts of area integral quantities, which do not isolate the correct geometry for this problem.

It's interesting that all the AI tools here are trying to conflate together two inconsistent decompositions to make the argument strategy work. In order to keep all the excess length coming from stitching together the decompositions under control, one can only remove a small neighborhood around the critical point (as depicted in this AI image, and in the incorrect long version of the AI paper). But in order to get the topological flow structure given by Lemma 8, one has to remove a much longer strip (the neighborhood of a lemniscate of Bernoulli (or more colloquially, the infinity symbol $\infty$), in the $z^2-r^2$ case), which solves the Lemma 8 problem but now creates a huge problem in trying to stitch together the pieces while keeping the length under control (this is not pictured in the AI image, most likely because the AI cannot figure out how to make this picture look at all plausible). I don't think either approach is salvageable with anything resembling the current strategy, but somehow the fact that both versions of the approach have a different half of the argument working seems to be enough to convince the AIs that the argument is fixable.

Thanks a lot for these comments. I appreciate these mathematical insights. However, throughout the conversations, GPT-5.4 Thinking did not become convinced that the argument was fixable. It only reasoned through according to the drafts and the picture you provided; please see the linked chat. The notion that there were two halves of the argument did not come up; GPT only treated what it understood to be the common strategy to both drafts. The keeping of the length under control was not discussed in the conversation and that was simply why it was not included in the picture.

Keep in mind that GPT-5.4 Thinking only reasoned about this objection: "The problem is that this flow simply does not create trees, which becomes obvious once one generates a visual plot." And the image was a response to that objection. For example, the circles are large because the topology would be the same but it's easier to see the structure that way. For some reason, I feel that its intention was greatly misread throughout. I also do not understand how it can be concluded that "all the AI tools here are trying to conflate..." when all these conversations are on different topics. Moreover, I think only a few conversations have both papers uploaded together. And in those cases, the notion that there were two halves of the argument did not come up; usually the longer draft was viewed as a (flawed) attempt to repair the shorter draft in a specific way.
In any case, I will now take your conclusion that the strategy is doomed to fail for granted, without investigating further.

I agree, after some more inspection I believe that even the statement of Prop 12, (not just the proof) is incorrect, and so this strategy is doomed to fail. I am exploring some more analytic alternatives, but have not had success yet. Thanks everyone for your comments!

About geometry/topology: anecdotally this seems about right. There's also some evidence on matharena using visual math test. Note though that some geometry/topology results can be clearly proven algebraically and anecdotally these seem to pose fewer problems of this kind. But yes, I agree with this observation in general. ( Architecturally, though, it's unclear. Current LLMs do have vision input, and some have vision *output*. For intermediate representations, some research has extracted topological structures out of them, so theoretically these may be usable for visualization. But anecdotally, such capability does not seem to have yet emerged. )

I did some discussion with GPT-5.4 Thinking. The story was a bit complex, but we're geared towards this conclusion: - The gap may be repairable. But this is not quite trivial partly because of the proof's length. - On the other hand, it appears that the gap may be due to a particular way that the paper attempts to the fix the earlier shorter draft. - Therefore, it appears plausible that the best strategy is to start over from the shorter draft and repair the (existing) issues there in a different, cleaner way. - There's some indication that this is plausible, but of course we're not fully confident. For shtuka: if you attempt this, I will of course review as usual. But because we've seen that this particular problem is tricky for GPT, I'll raise the bar and will only accept it as full solution in the wiki with another human's confirmation or something roughly similar.

( Personally, I do want to see the finished, correct argument because it really seems like an interesting argument! Unless of course this strategy is doomed to fail, but I haven't seen the reason for this yet if that is indeed the case. But I understand that the work could be complicated. In any case, in the wiki this will be in pending for a while, then if there's no correct solution found it'll be marked as having major gaps. )

After some back and forth with GPT 5.4, I was able to settle the general problem, double checked by Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1, and myself. Some comments on what exactly is proven: As @BorisAlexeev notes, the 0-length path in the case of roots with multiplicity is counted in this proof. Also, the result in [EPH58] regarding the existence of a connected component containing at least two of the roots is taken as a blackbox.
Chat GPT flags various issues. This argument may require significant human attention.
Thanks. Standard check claims 3 required issues that are plausibly repairable. As per Tao, revision is needed.

Very interesting, thanks for sharing your proof. 13 years ago I was interested in such paths not between two zeros of a polynomial f, but between one zero of f and one zero of its derivative f'. EDIT: I am working at transfering Shtuka's approach to get path length $\le 1$ for the $f -> f'$ transfer (instead of $\le 2$ in the #1041 problem). Here is a link to some diagrams from 2013 (the ones in the lower part of that site are more interesting): Diagrams and some German text By "Tanz der Nullstellen" I mean the picture from "Musical Chairs": n persons and n-1 chairs. The persons are the zeros of f. The chairs are the zeros of the derivative.

This is quite interesting. By Riemann-Hurwitz, a component of $\Lambda_f := \{z: |f(z)| < 1\}$ with $m$ zeros of $f$ will have $m-1$ zeros of $f'$. (Note: zeros must be counted with multiplicity.) The two proofs combined, if correct, appear to claim the following. Consider any component $U$ of $\Lambda_f$ with at least two roots of $f$. By [EHP58], such a component $U$ exists. Then: - There is a path in $U$ joining two roots of $f$ with length $< 2$. - There is a path in $U$ joining a root of $f$ and a root of $f'$ with length $< 1$. However, at present, both proofs at least require revision. I would encourage the authors here to work on it. Credit to GPT-5.4 Thinking for discussion.

Thanks for pointing to Riemann-Hurwitz. In my backhead I have some hope that (always or often) chains $z_i$ -> $y_j$ -> $x_k$ with "flat" paths and path lengths < 1 exist, where $z_i$ is a zero of polynomial $f$, $y_j$ a zero of derivative $f'$, $x_k$ a zero of the second derivative $f''$... ChatGPT gave a proof for cubic polynomials, but failed to do so for degree $\ge 4$. Gemini explained that Riemann-Hurwitz does not give chain structures. Nevertheless It would be nice to have "flat" paths - and I believe in "niceness" here. Perhaps someone has a more proof-friendly definition of "flat paths". Here is a link to some diagrams from the diploma thesis (2012) of Konrad Kaffka (Jena University), giving examples how root ensembles of $f, f', f'', f''' ...$ can behave. Kaffka Diagrams

Also, FYI, if we're not interested in the lemniscate $\{z: |f(z)| < 1\}$, *every* root of $f'$ is indeed within distance $< 1$ of some root of $f$. (Here all roots of $f$ lie in unit disk). Here is a solution using convex combination by GPT-5.4 Thinking.
Just a remark that the opposite problem of asking whether every root of $f$ is within distance $1$ of a root of $f'$ is a famous open conjecture of Sendov.

Sendov's Conjecture is interesting. Having in mind the "musical chair" picture for roots and critical points of polynomial $f$ one can look at the following variant of Sendov's: Let be $n \ge 1$. Let $f$ be a polynomial of degree n with zeros $z_1, ..., z_n$, all in the complex unit ball. Let $f'$ be the derivative (thus with degree $n-1$) with roots $y_1$, ..., $y_{n-1}$. Add the auxiliary point $y_n = 0$. ** Conjecture: Then there exists a permutation $p$ of {1, 2, ..., n} such that distance $(x_i, y_{p(i)}) \le 1$ for $i=1, ..., n$. The condition for $i=n$ holds trivially, because $0$ is central in the unit ball. The interesting remaining cases are $i=1, ..., n-1$. ** The conjecture is in some sense weaker than Sendov's, because $y_n$ gives a free meal for one root of $f$. But it is stronger than Sendov's in another sense, because all the other roots of $f$ need different near-distance $f'$-roots as their partners. For $n=3$ the conjecture has a simple proof by Hall's marriage theorem.

This is not a bad conjecture. It proposes that you can match all $n-1$ roots of $f'$ one-to-one with some $n-1$ roots of $f$ (leaving one out) so that the distance between each pair is $< 1$. GPT-5.4 Thinking does not recognize this as a named conjecture nor can it find it in literature. The case $n=3$ follows by Sendov's conjecture + the "opposite" version above + Hall's marriage theorem (or brute force enumeration). It's also true if all roots of $f$ are real because roots of $f'$ are between the consecutive roots of $f$. Randomizing roots does not easily give a counterexample. In principle, Sendov's conjecture and this matching variant can be proved or disproved for any specific $n$ with finite computation. GPT-5.4 Thinking attempts a geometric proof for the case $n=4$ but does not succeed.

Over on the Formal Conjectures project, Salvatore Mercuri notes that there is some intricacy with repeated roots. It appears that: for roots with multiplicity, the 0-length path connecting them should count for this problem.
Gemini Deep Research did not turn up any work on this problem (noting the contrast to #114 which has a significant literature).
